cards.definition: the smallest particle of an element — made of protons, neutrons, electrons
a substance made of only one type of atom — cannot be broken down chemically
two or more elements chemically bonded — different properties from its elements
two or more substances not chemically bonded — separated by physical methods
single element or compound with a fixed melting/boiling point
separates mixture components by different solubilities in a solvent
distance moved by spot ÷ distance moved by solvent front — identifies substances
total mass of reactants = total mass of products — atoms rearranged, not destroyed
sum of all relative atomic masses in a formula
unit of amount — 1 mole = 6.02 × 10²³ particles — moles = mass ÷ Mr

questions.question: What is a compound?
options: C) Two or more elements chemically bonded — different properties
questions.explanation: NaCl has very different properties from sodium (reactive metal) and chlorine (toxic gas).

questions.question: What is conservation of mass?
options: B) Mass decreases in exothermic reactions
C) Total mass of reactants = total mass of products — atoms rearranged, not destroyed
D) Only applies to physical changes
questions.explanation: Atoms are neither created nor destroyed — they are rearranged.

questions.question: What is an Rf value?
options: C) Distance moved by spot ÷ distance moved by solvent front
questions.explanation: Rf values between 0 and 1 — each substance has a characteristic Rf in a given solvent.

questions.question: What is a mole?
options: C) A unit of amount — 1 mole = 6.02 × 10²³ particles
questions.explanation: Moles = mass ÷ Mr. Links mass to number of particles.

questions.question: What is relative formula mass?
options: B) Sum of all atomic masses in a formula
C) Number of moles in a compound
questions.explanation: Mr = sum of all Ar values. E.g. H₂O: (2×1) + 16 = 18.

questions.question: What is chromatography used for?
options: C) Separating mixture components by different solubilities
questions.explanation: Components travel different distances depending on solubility in the solvent.

questions.question: What is a pure substance?
options: B) A substance with a fixed melting/boiling point — single element or compound
questions.explanation: A pure substance has a sharp, fixed melting point — impurities lower and broaden it.

questions.question: What is the difference between a mixture and a compound?
options: B) Compounds separated physically; mixtures cannot
C) Mixtures not chemically bonded — separated physically; compounds require chemical reactions
questions.explanation: In mixtures, substances retain their individual properties and can be separated physically.

questions.question: How are moles calculated?
questions.explanation: Moles = mass ÷ Mr. E.g. 18g of water (Mr = 18) = 1 mole.

questions.question: What is an element?
options: A) Two or more atoms bonded
C) A substance made of only one type of atom — cannot be chemically broken down
D) A compound with one element
questions.explanation: All atoms of an element have the same atomic number.

questions.question: A compound has different properties from its elements.
questions.explanation: NaCl (safe) ≠ Na (explosive) + Cl₂ (toxic).

questions.question: Mass is lost in chemical reactions.
questions.explanation: Conservation of mass: reactant mass = product mass.

questions.question: Rf values are between 0 and 1.
questions.explanation: Rf = distance by substance ÷ distance by solvent. Max = 1.

questions.question: One mole = 6.02 × 10²³ particles.
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant.

questions.question: A pure substance has a range of melting points.
questions.explanation: Pure substances have a FIXED, SHARP melting point.

questions.question: Mixtures can be separated by physical methods.
questions.explanation: Filtration, distillation, chromatography.

questions.question: Mr is the sum of all atomic masses in a formula.
Compounds can be separated by physical methods.
questions.explanation: Compounds require CHEMICAL reactions to separate.

questions.question: Moles = mass × Mr.
questions.explanation: Moles = mass ÷ Mr (divided, not multiplied).

questions.question: Chromatography separates by differences in solubility.
questions.explanation: Components travel different distances in the solvent.

questions.question: A ________ is two or more elements chemically bonded.
questions.explanation: Different properties from constituent elements.

questions.question: Conservation of ________: reactant mass = product mass.
questions.explanation: Atoms rearranged — not created or destroyed.

questions.question: ________ = distance by spot ÷ distance by solvent front.
questions.explanation: Between 0 and 1 — identifies substances in chromatography.

questions.question: A ________ has a fixed melting/boiling point.
questions.explanation: Impurities lower and broaden the melting point.

questions.question: ________ = mass ÷ Mr — unit of amount.
questions.explanation: 1 mole = 6.02 × 10²³ particles.

questions.question: Relative ________ mass = sum of all atomic masses in the formula.
________ separates components by different solubilities in a solvent.
questions.explanation: Used to identify unknown substances by Rf values.

questions.question: An ________ is one type of atom only — cannot be broken down chemically.
questions.explanation: 118 known elements in the periodic table.

questions.question: A ________ contains two or more substances NOT chemically bonded.
questions.explanation: Separated by physical methods.

questions.question: 1 mole contains 6.02 × 10²³ particles — ________'s constant.
questions.explanation: Links mass to number of particles.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: distance by spot ÷ distance by solvent — identifies substances

cards.definition: transfer of electrons — positive and negative ions attracted — e.g. NaCl
sharing of electron pairs between non-metals — e.g. H₂O, CO₂
positive ions in a sea of delocalised electrons — good conductor
regular 3D arrangement of oppositely charged ions — high melting point
covalent molecules with weak intermolecular forces — low melting point, no conductivity
network of covalent bonds — very high melting point — diamond, silicon dioxide
each C bonded to 4 others — very hard, high mp, no conductivity
each C bonded to 3 — layers slide — conducts electricity via delocalised electrons
long chain molecule of repeating monomer units — e.g. polythene from ethene
1–100 nm particles — very high surface area:volume ratio

questions.question: What is ionic bonding?
options: B) Transfer of electrons — positive and negative ions attracted
questions.explanation: Metal loses electrons (positive ion); non-metal gains (negative ion) → electrostatic attraction.

questions.question: What is covalent bonding?
options: B) Sharing electron pairs between non-metals
questions.explanation: Covalent bonds: non-metals share electron pairs to achieve full outer shells.

questions.question: Why do ionic compounds have high melting points?
options: B) Strong electrostatic attraction between many ions in the lattice
questions.explanation: Ionic lattices: strong electrostatic forces throughout — much energy needed to break apart.

questions.question: Why does graphite conduct electricity?
options: B) Delocalised electrons free to carry charge — one electron per carbon not in a bond
questions.explanation: Each carbon in graphite forms 3 bonds — one delocalised electron per carbon free to carry charge.

questions.question: Why do simple molecular compounds have low melting points?
options: B) Weak intermolecular forces between molecules — easily overcome
questions.explanation: Only weak intermolecular forces (not covalent bonds) need to be broken — little energy required.

questions.question: What is metallic bonding?
options: B) Positive metal ions in a sea of delocalised electrons
questions.explanation: Delocalised electrons carry charge and heat — explain electrical and thermal conductivity.

questions.question: What is a polymer?
options: B) Long chain molecule made of repeating monomers
questions.explanation: E.g. polythene made from many ethene monomers joined together.

questions.question: Why is diamond very hard?
options: C) Each carbon bonded to 4 others — rigid 3D covalent network
questions.explanation: All 4 outer electrons form bonds — completely rigid structure.

questions.question: What is a nanoparticle?
options: B) A particle 1–100 nm — very high SA:V ratio
questions.explanation: Very high SA:V gives unique properties compared to bulk materials.

questions.question: What is the difference between diamond and graphite?
options: B) Diamond: 4 bonds per C, no conductivity; graphite: 3 bonds per C, conducts
questions.explanation: Diamond: tetrahedral 4-bond structure, very hard, no free electrons. Graphite: layered, one delocalised electron per C.

questions.question: Ionic bonding involves sharing electrons.
questions.explanation: Ionic bonding involves TRANSFER — covalent bonding involves sharing.

questions.question: Ionic compounds conduct when solid.
questions.explanation: Conduct only when MOLTEN or DISSOLVED — ions need to be free.

questions.question: Simple molecular compounds have high melting points.
questions.explanation: LOW melting points — weak intermolecular forces.

questions.question: Graphite conducts due to delocalised electrons.
questions.explanation: One delocalised electron per carbon.

questions.question: Nanoparticles have a low SA:V ratio.
questions.explanation: Nanoparticles have VERY HIGH SA:V ratio.

questions.question: Metallic bonding explains electrical conductivity.
questions.explanation: Delocalised electrons carry charge.

questions.question: Diamond is soft because bonds between layers are weak.
questions.explanation: Diamond has NO layers — rigid 3D network. Graphite has weak forces between layers.

questions.question: Polymers are made of repeating monomer units.
questions.explanation: Polymerisation joins many monomers into long chains.

questions.question: Covalent bonds involve electron transfer.
questions.explanation: Covalent bonds involve SHARING — transfer is ionic.

questions.question: Giant covalent structures have very high melting points.
questions.explanation: Many strong covalent bonds must be broken.

questions.question: ________ bonding: electron transfer — positive and negative ions attract.
questions.explanation: Metal → positive; non-metal → negative.

questions.question: ________ bonding: sharing electron pairs between non-metals.
questions.explanation: Shared electrons give both atoms full outer shells.

questions.question: ________ bonding: positive ions in sea of delocalised electrons.
questions.explanation: Explains conductivity and malleability.

questions.question: ________ has 4 bonds per C — rigid — hard — no conductivity.
questions.explanation: All 4 electrons in bonds — none free.

questions.question: ________ has 3 bonds per C — layered — conducts electricity.
questions.explanation: One delocalised electron per carbon.

questions.question: Simple ________ structures have low melting points — weak intermolecular forces.
questions.explanation: Covalent bonds not broken — only weak forces.

questions.question: ________ covalent structures have very high melting points.
questions.explanation: E.g. diamond, silicon dioxide.

questions.question: A ________ is a long chain of repeating monomer units.
questions.explanation: E.g. polythene from ethene.

questions.question: ________ are 1–100 nm particles — very high SA:V ratio.
questions.explanation: Used in medicine, sunscreens, electronics.

questions.question: An ionic ________ has high melting point — strong electrostatic forces throughout.
questions.explanation: Regular 3D arrangement of oppositely charged ions.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: 4 bonds per C — hard — no conductivity

cards.definition: produces H⁺ ions in solution — pH below 7
soluble base — produces OH⁻ ions — pH above 7
acid + base → salt + water
how fast a reaction occurs — affected by temperature, concentration, surface area, catalyst
particles must collide with ≥ activation energy — more collisions = faster rate
speeds up reaction by lowering activation energy — not consumed
releases heat — temperature of surroundings rises — e.g. combustion, neutralisation
absorbs heat — temperature of surroundings falls — e.g. thermal decomposition
can go forwards and backwards — ⇌ symbol
forward and reverse rates equal — concentrations constant

questions.question: What is neutralisation?
options: A) Dissolving a metal in acid
questions.explanation: H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O. Acid + base always gives salt and water.

questions.question: What does collision theory state?
options: A) All collisions lead to reactions
B) Particles must collide with ≥ activation energy for a reaction
C) More particles always means faster reactions
questions.explanation: Successful collisions need correct orientation AND sufficient energy.

questions.question: What is a catalyst?
options: B) Speeds up reaction by lowering activation energy — not consumed
C) A product of the reaction
questions.explanation: Catalysts provide an alternative lower-energy pathway — more collisions are successful.

questions.question: What is exothermic?
questions.explanation: Exothermic: energy released. E.g. combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is dynamic equilibrium?
options: B) Forward and reverse rates equal — concentrations stay constant
C) All reactants converted to products
questions.explanation: At dynamic equilibrium both directions continue — rates are equal, not concentrations.

questions.question: How does temperature affect rate?
options: B) Higher temperature: more collisions with sufficient energy — faster rate
questions.explanation: Higher temp → more kinetic energy → more frequent and energetic collisions → faster rate.

questions.question: What is an acid?
options: B) Produces H⁺ in solution — pH below 7
D) Any substance dissolving in water
questions.explanation: Strong acids fully ionise (HCl); weak acids partially ionise (CH₃COOH).

questions.question: What is formed when acid reacts with carbonate?
questions.explanation: Acid + carbonate → salt + water + CO₂.

questions.question: What is an endothermic reaction?
options: B) Absorbs heat from surroundings — temperature drops
questions.explanation: Endothermic: temperature of surroundings drops. E.g. thermal decomposition.

questions.question: What is a reversible reaction?
options: B) Can proceed forwards and backwards — shown by ⇌
D) A reaction that cannot reach equilibrium
questions.explanation: Reversible reactions: products can reform reactants. Reach dynamic equilibrium in a closed system.

questions.question: Neutralisation: acid + base → salt + water.
questions.explanation: H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O.

questions.question: Catalysts are consumed in reactions.
questions.explanation: Catalysts lower activation energy — not consumed.

questions.question: Exothermic reactions absorb heat.
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions RELEASE heat — temperature rises.

questions.question: Increasing temperature increases reaction rate.
questions.explanation: More kinetic energy → more successful collisions.

questions.question: At equilibrium the reaction has stopped.
questions.explanation: At equilibrium forward and reverse rates are equal — reaction continues.

questions.question: Acids produce H⁺ ions in solution.
questions.explanation: Strong acids fully ionise; weak acids partially ionise.

questions.question: Acid + carbonate → salt + water + hydrogen.
questions.explanation: Acid + carbonate → salt + water + CARBON DIOXIDE.

questions.question: Endothermic reactions show a temperature decrease.
questions.explanation: Energy absorbed from surroundings → surroundings cool.

questions.question: Catalysts increase activation energy.
questions.explanation: Catalysts LOWER activation energy.

questions.question: Reversible reactions use the ⇌ symbol.
questions.explanation: The double arrow shows the reaction can go in both directions.

questions.question: ________ + base → salt + water.
questions.explanation: H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O.

questions.question: ________ theory: particles must collide with ≥ activation energy.
questions.explanation: Correct orientation AND sufficient energy required.

questions.question: A ________ lowers activation energy — not consumed.
questions.explanation: Provides alternative lower-energy pathway.

questions.question: ________ reactions release heat — temperature rises.
questions.explanation: Combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: ________ reactions absorb heat — temperature falls.
questions.explanation: Thermal decomposition.

questions.question: Higher ________ means more particles have ≥ activation energy — faster rate.
questions.explanation: More kinetic energy → more successful collisions.

questions.question: ________ equilibrium: forward and reverse rates equal — concentrations constant.
questions.explanation: Reaction has NOT stopped — both directions continue.

questions.question: ________ reactions can go forwards and backwards — shown by ⇌.
questions.explanation: Reach dynamic equilibrium in a closed system.

questions.question: Acid + carbonate → salt + water + ________.
questions.explanation: CO₂ can be tested with limewater.

questions.question: An ________ is a substance producing H⁺ ions — pH below 7.
questions.explanation: Strong acids fully ionise; weak acids partially ionise.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

blocks.block_name: Separate Science: Quantitative Chemistry & Organic Chemistry

cards.definition: actual yield ÷ theoretical yield × 100% — always ≤ 100%
Mr of desired product ÷ sum of Mr of all products × 100%
the reactant that runs out first — determines maximum product
moles ÷ volume (mol/dm³) — amount of solute in a given volume
equilibrium shifts to oppose any imposed change
saturated hydrocarbons — CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ — only single C-C bonds
unsaturated hydrocarbons — CₙH₂ₙ — contain C=C double bond
reaction across a C=C double bond — e.g. bromine water decolourised by alkenes
thermal or catalytic breaking of long alkane chains — produces shorter alkanes and alkenes
many alkene monomers join together — e.g. ethene → polythene

questions.question: What is percentage yield?
questions.explanation: Always ≤ 100% — practical losses from reversible reactions, product loss, impure reactants.

questions.question: What is atom economy?
options: A) Number of atoms in reactant
B) Mr of desired product ÷ sum of Mr of all products × 100%
questions.explanation: High atom economy = more atoms in desired product = less waste = more sustainable.

questions.question: What is a limiting reactant?
options: B) Reactant always completely used regardless
C) Reactant that runs out first — determines maximum product
questions.explanation: Once the limiting reactant is used up, the reaction stops.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's principle?
options: B) Temperature always favours forward reaction
C) Equilibrium shifts to oppose any imposed change
D) Pressure only affects solid equilibria
questions.explanation: Increase concentration → shift to use it up. Increase temperature → shift endothermic. Increase pressure → shift to fewer moles of gas.

questions.question: What is the difference between alkanes and alkenes?
options: B) Alkanes: only single C-C bonds (saturated); alkenes: contain C=C double bond (unsaturated)
C) Alkenes have only single bonds
D) Alkanes are liquids; alkenes are gases
questions.explanation: Alkanes: CₙH₂ₙ₊₂, saturated. Alkenes: CₙH₂ₙ, unsaturated, reactive C=C double bond.

questions.question: How do you test for an alkene?
options: B) Burn and test for CO₂
C) Add bromine water — decolourises if alkene present
questions.explanation: Alkenes decolourise orange bromine water by an addition reaction across the C=C bond.

questions.question: What is cracking?
options: B) Breaking long alkane chains into shorter chains and alkenes
questions.explanation: Thermal or catalytic cracking produces shorter, more useful alkane fractions and alkene monomers.

questions.question: What is addition polymerisation?
options: A) Monomers joined with water released
B) Many alkene monomers joining together — C=C opens — long polymer chain formed
D) Adding monomers to a solution
questions.explanation: E.g. many ethene molecules → polythene. The C=C double bonds open and join — no other products.

questions.question: What is concentration in chemistry?
options: B) Amount of solute per unit volume — moles ÷ volume (mol/dm³)
questions.explanation: Concentration = moles ÷ volume (dm³). Units: mol/dm³.

questions.question: What is an addition reaction?
options: B) Reaction adding atoms across a C=C double bond
C) Breaking a C-C single bond
D) A type of polymerisation only
questions.explanation: Addition reactions: small molecules add across the C=C — e.g. H₂ added (hydrogenation), Br₂ added (bromine water test).

questions.question: Percentage yield is always ≤ 100%.
questions.explanation: Practical losses mean 100% yield is never achieved.

questions.question: High atom economy produces more waste.
questions.explanation: HIGH atom economy = LESS waste.

questions.question: The limiting reactant is in the largest amount.
questions.explanation: Limiting reactant runs out FIRST — may not be in largest amount.

questions.question: Le Chatelier: equilibrium shifts to reinforce disturbances.
questions.explanation: Equilibrium shifts to OPPOSE disturbances.

questions.question: Alkenes are saturated hydrocarbons.
questions.explanation: Alkenes are UNSATURATED — they contain a C=C double bond. Alkanes are saturated.

questions.question: Bromine water tests for alkenes.
questions.explanation: Alkenes decolourise orange bromine water — addition reaction across C=C.

questions.question: Cracking produces alkenes.
questions.explanation: Cracking breaks long alkanes — producing shorter chains and reactive alkenes.

questions.question: Addition polymerisation releases water.
questions.explanation: Addition polymerisation: no water released — all atoms from monomers end up in the polymer.

questions.question: Concentration = moles ÷ volume.
questions.explanation: Units: mol/dm³.

questions.question: Alkanes have the formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂.
questions.explanation: Saturated hydrocarbons — only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: ________ yield = actual ÷ theoretical × 100% — always ≤ 100%.
questions.explanation: Practical losses prevent 100% yield.

questions.question: ________ economy = Mr of desired product ÷ total Mr × 100%.
questions.explanation: High atom economy = sustainable = less waste.

questions.question: The ________ reactant runs out first — determines maximum product.
questions.explanation: Other reactants remain in excess.

questions.question: Le ________'s principle: equilibrium shifts to oppose changes.
questions.explanation: Increase pressure → shift to fewer moles of gas.

questions.question: ________ are saturated hydrocarbons — CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ — only single bonds.
questions.explanation: Methane, ethane, propane.

questions.question: ________ are unsaturated hydrocarbons — CₙH₂ₙ — contain C=C.
questions.explanation: Ethene, propene.

questions.question: ________ water decolourises in the presence of an alkene — addition reaction.
questions.explanation: Tests for C=C double bond.

questions.question: ________ breaks long alkane chains → shorter chains + alkenes.
questions.explanation: Thermal or catalytic.

questions.question: Addition ________ joins many alkene monomers — C=C opens — long chain.
questions.explanation: E.g. ethene → polythene. No by-products.

questions.question: ________ = moles ÷ volume — measured in mol/dm³.
questions.explanation: Volume must be in dm³.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.
